summary: Added OwloryAccessibilityContrast helper enum in AppTheme.swift (next to OwloryMotion) with tintedFill(_:alpha:reduceTransparency:increasedContrast:), tintedBorder(_:alpha:reduceTransparency:increasedContrast:), and borderWidth(_:increasedContrast:). Applied at finding M09 (Train status pill: background + border alpha + border width all gate on accessibilityReduceTransparency / colorSchemeContrast) and at the state-conveying M06 site in TodayView (stale-day badge warning capsule). TodayView and TrainView's SessionCardView each gained two new @Environment reads. The other M06 sites are decorative .opacity(0.8) icon tints with no state-conveying role and are explicitly named as out-of-scope. M08 (readiness color severity tint) is paired with positional shape in the 5-dot picker and was confirmed already-paired without needing a fix.
validations_passed: manual smoke: xcrun simctl install + launch on iPhone 17 Pro Max — app process running (PID 59222), no init-time crash

sites_modified.what: Status pill (Planned/Completed/Skipped/Modified) — background alpha 0.15 + border alpha 0.3 + border width 1 all now route through OwloryAccessibilityContrast helpers. Under reduceTransparency the alpha floors raise (background 0.35, border 0.55); under colorSchemeContrast == .increased the color becomes solid and border width doubles.
Stale-day badge (Continue row indicator: 'Xd' showing how many days an item has been carried) — warning tint alpha 0.12 gated. This is state-conveying (carried-forward signal) so it earned the gate.

sites_explicitly_not_modified.why: All are .foregroundStyle(OwloryColor.brandPrimary.opacity(0.8)) on caption / icon foreground tints — decorative visual hierarchy, not state-conveying. Reduce Transparency does not affect text legibility for opacity 0.8.

Brand-primary 0.10 fill highlights a momentarily-tapped continue row. The highlight is transient and the row content (icon + title + domain) carries the identity cue; the tint is purely a brief 'you just tapped this' accent. Gating it would add complexity for negligible accessibility gain.

The function returns OwloryColor.error/warning/success/textTertiary based on a numeric severity score. The consumer is the 5-dot readiness picker: each dot's POSITION conveys the same level as the color. Confirmed already-paired with positional shape; no color-only state signal.

design_decisions: Two distinct floors for reduceTransparency: 0.35 for fills (legible without solidifying), 0.55 for borders (sharper visual edge). Under increasedContrast both go to solid (alpha = 1.0) plus border width is doubled.
Did not modify foregroundStyle opacities. SwiftUI text rendering at 0.8 alpha is essentially unaffected by Reduce Transparency; only background tints + glass effects are.

Did not gate AppTheme.ContinueHighlightModifier in this slice. It's a momentary visual cue and would require passing env values through scrollToContinueHighlight's callers, expanding the slice unnecessarily. Named as a follow-up if/when the pattern duplicates.

Helper lives next to OwloryMotion in AppTheme.swift — the pbxproj-can't-find-new-files constraint applies here too. The MARK separator (Accessibility Contrast vs Accessibility Motion) keeps the file readable.
behavior_proven: xcodebuild build → exit 0; no compile errors.
App launches and runs on iPhone 17 Pro Max simulator with the marketing seed args (PID 59222 in launchctl list).
Existing visuals are preserved under default settings: when reduceTransparency=false and colorSchemeContrast=.standard, the helper returns the requested alpha unchanged.
Architecture lint + automation tests (124) + pyright + diff-check all green.
gaps_named_for_follow_up: On-device visual verification under Reduce Transparency ON and Increased Contrast ON. The build passes and the helpers are typed correctly; the user-visible color shift requires running on a device with the settings toggled in Settings -> Accessibility.

AppTheme.ContinueHighlightModifier — not gated in this slice. If a future audit shows the highlight tint is hard to see under reduceTransparency, the modifier can accept Bool params or use @Environment reads inside a SwiftUI view modifier (not the current closure-based modifier struct).

OwloryColor tokens themselves (brandPrimary, success, warning, error, etc.) are not currently differentiated by colorScheme contrast. Catalog-asset color sets COULD ship distinct .high-contrast variants. Out of scope for this slice.
not_claimed: Every transparency-sensitive UI surface in Owlory adapts. Only the two highest-impact state-conveying sites (Train status pill, stale-day badge) are gated.
The helper produces optimal contrast ratios under every accessibility configuration. The 0.35/0.55 alpha floors are reasonable defaults; a polish pass with WCAG ratio verification could refine.
Real-device behavior matches simulator. The proof level is build-tested + simulator-launch smoke.
residual_risks: If a future view adopts opacity-based state without going through OwloryAccessibilityContrast, it won't adapt. The pattern is opt-in.

The helper returns SwiftUI Color and trusts the caller to pass it through .background/.fill/.foregroundStyle/etc. Inserting it in a position where SwiftUI expects ShapeStyle vs Color could need .fill wrapping; the two sites modified in this slice both work with the Color type.
recommended_next_reason: Final slice in the accessibility-survey follow-up chain (pri 82). Adds short verb-noun aliases via .accessibilityInputLabels for high-frequency Voice Control commands. Larger blast radius than the others due to 19-locale fan-out for the new *.voicecontrol.label.* namespace.
